narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 35, you've clearly put in the work to build a competitive profile, including your middle family income background and plans to attend full-time. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 40.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school: your Year 3 decile of 2 was the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it stood out well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class. Closely balancing that was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked strongly in your favor by exceeding the average of 3.21 and pulling the prediction toward success. Your LSAT score, slightly below the usual 36.7, also helped by nudging things positively, much like your family income and full-time status, which aligned with common successful profiles. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3 had a milder but still negative influence, reinforcing concerns from your earlier performance. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later-year grades ranked lower than most, which overshadowed your stronger undergrad record and test scores. I know this must feel disappointing, especially when you've shown real strengths in key areas. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your undergrad transcript if possible, or seeking programs that weigh recent improvements more heavily. You might also explore post-baccalaureate courses to demonstrate upward academic momentum. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps—your profile has a strong foundation, and with targeted steps, you can improve your odds for the next application cycle. Keep pushing; your determination will pay off.
